coredata.dc:title: The construction of S-scheme heterojunction between P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 to enhance the synergistic degradation of levofloxacin by photo-Fenton oxidation

coredata.dc:description: In this paper, ternary nanocomposites (P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4) with S-scheme heterojunction was successfully composited by anchoring Fe3O4 onto nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs)-modified protonated graphitic carbon-nitride (P-CN) with the aid of hydrothermal and co-precipitation methods. This composite material exhibited efficient photocatalytic degradation ability towards the antibiotic levofloxacin (LEV), with a 5.6-fold degradation performance than that of CN and good cycling stability. The rapid transfer of photo-generated electrons through heterojunction not only reduced the adsorbed O₂ on the catalyst surface to generate •O2 -, but also oxidized H2O to generate •OH. At the same time, it improved the cycling of Fe (III) and Fe (II), and further enhanced the photocatalytic degradation effect of LEV. More importantly, the ternary structure achieved sound charge separation, which was the key to improving the photocatalytic efficiency. The internal electric field generated at the interface of Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs through an S-scheme heterojunction, which synergistically promoted photocatalysis and Fenton catalysis, improved the spatial separation and transfer characteristics of carriers, and enhanced the degradation effect of photo-Fenton and increased the degradation of antibiotics. This study not only deepens the understanding of the catalytic mechanism but also provides new strategies and methods to solve the problem of antibiotic contamination.

head.ce:title: The construction of S-scheme heterojunction between P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 to enhance the synergistic degradation of levofloxacin by photo-Fenton oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, ternary nanocomposites (P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4) with S-scheme heterojunction was successfully composited by anchoring Fe3O4 onto nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs)-modified protonated graphitic carbon-nitride (P-CN) with the aid of hydrothermal and co-precipitation methods. This composite material exhibited efficient photocatalytic degradation ability towards the antibiotic levofloxacin (LEV), with a 5.6-fold degradation performance than that of CN and good cycling stability. The rapid transfer of photo-generated electrons through heterojunction not only reduced the adsorbed O₂ on the catalyst surface to generate •O2 -, but also oxidized H2O to generate •OH. At the same time, it improved the cycling of Fe (III) and Fe (II), and further enhanced the photocatalytic degradation effect of LEV. More importantly, the ternary structure achieved sound charge separation, which was the key to improving the photocatalytic efficiency. The internal electric field generated at the interface of Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs through an S-scheme heterojunction, which synergistically promoted photocatalysis and Fenton catalysis, improved the spatial separation and transfer characteristics of carriers, and enhanced the degradation effect of photo-Fenton and increased the degradation of antibiotics. This study not only deepens the understanding of the catalytic mechanism but also provides new strategies and methods to solve the problem of antibiotic contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Levofloxacin (LEV), as a third-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotics, is the most widely used worldwide. Currently, the ecological problems caused by the abuse of fluoroquinolone antibiotics are very prominent, with a certain degree of damage to agriculture [1], soil [2], and aquatic organisms [3], and even serious potential harm to human beings. Usually, levofloxacin is mainly removed by anaerobic digestion [4], physical adsorption [5], and membrane separation [6], but they present several shortcomings such as low degradation efficiency, high cost, and secondary pollution.

The removal of persistent organic pollutants in water through semiconductor photocatalytic technology will become a green solution to the future environmental crisis. Graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) attracts much attention in the field of photocatalysis due to its suitable band gap, high stability, and simple preparation method [7]. However, the small specific surface area and narrow absorption range of visible light also limit its catalytic activity [8]. Modifications such as anchoring [9] and heterostructure construction [10,11] can improve photocatalytic performance, and the latter has an important impact on the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency. The energy level distribution of electrons and holes can be changed by constructing CN heterojunctions to facilitate carrier separation and transport. This not only inhibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers but also improves the redox capacity [12–15]. Quantum dots (QDs) have been used in solar cells, wastewater treatment, and other fields due to their special quantum size effect, electron transfer properties, and effective absorption of visible light [16,17]. The research found that the recombination between quantum dots and CN can broaden their light absorption range, reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers, and improve the photocatalytic efficiency to make up for the low light utilization rate of CN [18]. Some works showed that CN/NCDs have better photocatalytic performance for sulfamethoxazole and tetracycline in water [19–21]. However, several problems still exist such as the universality of this type of photocatalyst in practical applications and the improvement of the recovery rate. Therefore, it is necessary to improve the catalytic performance while solving the above problems [22]. Metal oxides are cheap, stable, safe, and widely used semiconductor materials. The performance of their photocatalysts is mainly determined by the design of their energy band structures [23–25]. The construction of oxide semiconductor heterojunctions with optimized energy band structures can not only adjust the energy band structure to make full use of optical energy, but also generate a built-in electric field at the interface of the heterojunction to increase the utilization rate of photogenerated electrons [26,27]. As an important magnetic semiconductor material, Fe3O4 has the advantages of low toxicity, simple preparation, easy separation, recycling, absorption of all-optical spectrum light in the solar spectrum, and so on [23–25]. As a special Fenton catalyst, it can accelerate the reaction between photogenerated electrons (e-) and Fe3+. Besides, the catalyst modified by Fe3O4 not only has excellent photocatalytic performance but also shows sound magnetic recovery ability [26–28]. For example, the composite ZnIn2S4@Fe3O4 designed by Liang et al. have been widely used in the treatment of organic pollutants [29]. Based on the above research, we assumed that Fe3O4 and binary composite photocatalysts (P-CN/NCDs) can successfully be composited to form heterojunctions, and the heterojunction interface can serve as a strong conductive platform for photo-induced electron transport from P-CN/NCDs to Fe3O4. The ultimate goal is to improve the photocatalytic degradation performance of composite materials for organic pollutants.

In this paper, a new kind of ternary nanocomposite (P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4) based on S-scheme heterojunction was designed and synthesized by introducing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) and Fe3O4 nanoparticles in a stepwise manner with graphitic carbon nitride (P-CN) as the substrate ( Scheme 1). The ternary nanocomposite was characterized by XRD, FT-IR, TG, SEM, XPS, and UV–vis DRS. The performance of the photocatalyst for antibiotic removal was evaluated under visible light. The high performance was mainly attributed to the synergistic heterojunction between Fe3O4, NCDs and P-CN, which facilitated the photo-induced electron transfer. In addition, the possible mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction was studied deeply and systematically based on photoluminescence (PL), photocurrent, and electron spin resonance (EPR) analyses. A new strategy for the development of more stable and photosensitive composites applied to solve the problem of antibiotic contamination is provided in this work.

P-CN/NCDs composites were prepared by hydrothermal method. 1 g of P-CN and a certain amount of NCDs were mixed in certain proportions (0, 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 % of the mass of P-CN). They were stirred for 10 min, injected into an autoclave, stored at 180 °C for 5 h, filtered and dried to obtain the binary composite photocatalyst P-CN/NCDs series products (labelled as P-CN/NCD-Xw%). The preparation process of other materials such as P-CN, NCDs was detailed in the supporting information.

FeCl3•6 H2O (0.2 mmol) and FeCl2•4 H2O (0.1 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of P-CN/NCDs (0.5 g) suspension, stirred at 90 ℃ for 30 min, and 2 mL of ammonia was rapidly injected into the above reactants, stirred for another 30 min, and then filtered and dried by filtration, washing and drying to obtain the P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 photocatalyst. The different contents of Fe3O4 could be changed by changing the addition ratios of FeCl2•4 H2O and FeCl3•6 H2O. The specific preparation of ternary composites P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 containing different contents of Fe3O4 was described in the Supporting Information. The P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4-s was made by direct physical mixing of pure Fe3O4 with P-CN/NCDs.

50 mg of catalyst was added to 50 mL, 50 mg/L solution of LEV (levofloxacin), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), respectively. After the dark reaction reached the adsorption equilibrium state, the metal halide lamp was used as a light source to start the light reaction. The concentration of the solution was quantified at the maximum absorption wavelengths (λmax) 294 nm, 319 nm, 364 nm, 365 nm, and 261 nm for LEV, CTC, OTC, TC, and TCH using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the solution was calculated by the following equation

The crystal structures of different samples were investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1a, the characteristic peaks at 2θ = 13.0º corresponded to the (100) plane of P-CN (d = 0.676 nm) and the (002) plane (d = 0.325 nm) at 2θ = 27.3° [30]. The characteristic peaks of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 at 2θ = 35.7º, 57.3º, and 62.9º were consistent with PDF#No.82–1533 (Fig. S1a). The newly appeared peaks in the XRD patterns of the ternary nanocomposites were attributed to the trans-spinel structure of Fe3O4 and the presence of the peaks confirmed the successful establishment of the structure [31]. The inability to determine the diffraction peaks of NCDs resulted from the relatively low dose of NCDs, which was similar to the results of previously published studies [32]. There were no changes in the positions of the representative peaks of P-CN and Fe3O4, indicating that there were no other new phases in the XRD patterns of the heterojunction composites. Therefore, it can be concluded that a composite of P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4 was formed. Combined with the degradation experiments and the later XPS analysis, it could be seen that the ternary nanophotocatalyst P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was successfully composited. Due to the presence of Fe3O4, we can consider that both Fe(III) and Fe(II) exist in the novel composite P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4, and the electrons can be transferred between Fe(III) and Fe(II) [33]. The newly synthesized composite P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was found to be superparamagnetic, which facilitated the recycling of the catalyst. It could also be used as a redox medium for collecting and consuming electrons, which was used to improve the separation rate of photogenerated carriers. The peaks of both P-CN/NCDs and P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 at 2θ = 13.0º and 2θ = 27.3º shifted toward lower angles (Fig. S1b) (2θ = 27.3º∼27.2º∼27.2º, 2θ = 13.0º∼12.0 º∼11.8º), and the decrease in the diffraction angle indicated an increase in the interlayer distance [34]. This possibly resulted from the disruption of the hydrogen-bond reaction of the aromatic system in the P-CN plane generated by the dispersion of trace amounts of NCDs and Fe3O4 [35,36], which indicated a close contact between Fe3O4, NCDs and P-CN. This close contact resulted in better performance of the ternary photocatalysts for charge transfer and separation in photocatalysis. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 1b) showed that P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 retained the main structure of P-CN well (Fig. S2a), which suggested that the original structure was not affected after the formation of the heterojunction composite. Besides, as the Fe coordinated with the O and N elements in the P-CN/NCDs to further regulate the surface electronic structure of the material, the introduction of Fe3O4 resulted in new absorption peaks at 1128 cm−1 and 645 cm−1 (Fig. S2b), which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of Fe-N and Fe-O [37]. Among them, the Fe-O stretching vibration might enhance the surface -OH absorption peaks, thus increased the density of hydroxyl groups on the surface of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 and improved the adsorption properties of the composites [38,39]. These characterizations also confirmed the successful preparation of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 composites. Compared with the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of P-CN and the corresponding BJH pore diameter distribution (Fig. S3a), P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 (Fig. 1c) showed a type IV isotherm characteristic, indicating a mesoporous structure [19]. The surface area of BET was 54.06 m2/g, about 2.8 times that of P-CN ( Table 1), indicating that the newly synthesized ternary nanomaterial P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 could improve the adsorption capacity of pollutants. In general, the adsorption of contaminants on the catalyst surface is a prerequisite for photocatalytic and photo-Fenton catalytic reactions. The pore size distribution curves also showed that the pores of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were mainly distributed in the range of 5–15 nm, whereas the pores of P-CN/NCDs were much larger (10–50 nm) (Fig. S3b). This demonstrated that the introduction of Fe3O4 reduced the pores of the composite, but the experiments illustrated its better photocatalytic performance, which was attributed to the multilayered pore structure of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4. This facilitated the exposure of more reactive active sites, increased the mass transfer rate, and improved the catalytic performance of the material. It is further illustrated that the formation of heterojunctions between Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs improved carrier separation and migration, contributing to the accelerated transfer of electrons and facilitating the regeneration of Fe(II) by Fe(III) reduction.

In general, the change in binding energy of XPS spectra is related to the electron migration pathway of the heterojunction. In this case, the shift of peaks to higher binding energies indicates a weakening of the electron density effect, while the shift of peaks to lower binding energies indicates an enhancement of the electron density effect [18]. The surface composition and chemical structure of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were analyzed by XPS. The peak centers of the fitted C 1 s spectra ( Fig. 2a) appeared at 284.80 eV (C-C (sp2)), 286.10 eV (CC) and 288.22 eV (N-CN). Compared with P-CN/Fe3O4, the CC peak of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was shifted to a lower binding energy by 0.05 eV, which might be due to the increase in the local electron density of P-CN after the introduction of NCDs [40,41]. The peak centers of the fitted N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2b) appeared at 398.25 eV [pyridine nitrogen (CN-C)], 399.30 eV [graphitic nitrogen (N- (C)3)], 400.62 eV [pyrroline nitrogen (C-NH-C)], respectively. And the peaks of N 1 s and C 1 s in P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were shifted to lower binding energies than P-CN/NCDs, suggesting that there was an interaction between Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs, and electrons tended to be transferred from P-CN/NCDs to Fe3O4. This was attributed to the coordination of P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4 to the d-orbitals of Fe through the provision of lone pairs of electrons by P-CN [42]. These results further confirmed the formation of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 heterojunctions [43]. The peak centers of the fitted O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2c) appeared at 529.79 eV 531.61 eV (C-O) and 532.74 eV (CO). The peak at 529.79 eV could be attributed to Fe-O, indicating that iron was connected to the carbon atoms of P-CN/NCDs via Fe-O, which was consistent with the above FT-IR results [44]. The characteristics further indicated that the novel composite P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was successfully prepared. 710.07 eV and 711.70 eV corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively(Fig. 2d). Thus, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 contains Fe3O4, while the Fe elements were mainly Fe2+ and Fe3+ respectively. The positions of the C 1 s, N 1 s and O1s peaks in P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were shifted toward lower binding energies while Fe 2p was shifted toward higher binding energies also suggesting an interaction between them[41]. It indicated that Fe2+ and Fe3+ exist in the new composite material P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4, while electrons could be transferred between Fe2+ and Fe3+ [45]. In conclusion, the binding energy of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was larger than that of P-CN/NCDs, which was attributed to the fact that the pyridine N in the structure of P-CN/NCDs possesses a lone pair of electrons. This electron could be transferred to the neighboring Fe3O4, leading to an increase in the electron density of Fe(III) [46,47]. These results suggested that there were some contacts between P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4 to form heterojunctions in P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 photocatalysts.

Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were shifted to lower binding energies compared with those of pure Fe3O4, P-CN/NCDs, P-CN/Fe3O4 and P-CN. It indicated that there was a strong electronic interaction between P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4. Moreover, XPS patterns obtained after metal halide lamp at room temperature (Fig. S5) showed that the N-CN, N-(C)3 and C-NH-C binding energies of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were shifted to the lower energy levels, while the O 1 s and Fe 2p binding energies were shifted to the higher energy levels. This might be due to the light-induced electron migration from Fe3O4 to P-CN/NCDs. The XPS analyses further confirmed the successful preparation and formation of tightly bonded interfaces of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4, which was favourable for the electron migration to form an internal electric field.

The large shift in the binding energy was attributed to the electronic interactions that occurred during the experiments, which proved the formation of heterojunctions between P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4 [48]. The morphology of the samples was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Compared with P-CN (Fig. S6) the surface structure of P-CN/NCDs was very rough (Fig. S7a). The surface of P-CN/NCDs was covered by a large number of spherical particles (average particle size of about 3 nm) with the introduction of NCDs. A lattice stripe spacing of 0.238 nm was observed from the S7c plot, which could be used as supporting evidence for successful complexation of NCDs [32,35]. In Fig. S7d, a bright diffraction ring corresponding to the (110) plane of the NCDs could be seen [32]. With the continued introduction of Fe3O4, the surface of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was rougher ( Fig. 3a-b). Further observation of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 by transmission electron microscopy revealed a large number of particles (with an average particle size of about 8.2 nm) attached to the P-CN/NCDs structure (Fig. 3c-d). Analyzed by HRTEM (Fig. 3e-g), it could be seen from Fig. 3g that Fe3O4 nanoparticles (circled with green dashed lines) well-modified P-CN/NCDs. It could be seen from the Fig. 3g that the lattice spacing d = 0.263 nm corresponds to the (311) plane of Fe3O4 and d = 0.238 nm corresponds to the (110) plane of NCDs [30]. In Fig. 3h, two diffraction rings were found, corresponding to the (311) and (331) planes of Fe3O4, which further indicated the successful recombination of Fe3O4 [30]. The morphology of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 (Fig. 3e-g) showed tightly bound interfaces, which might favor electron migration. The elemental mapping of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 (Fig. 3i) further indicated that NCDs and Fe3O4 have been successfully introduced into the composite. There was enough interfacial contact between them for rapid charge transfer during the reaction. The EDS diagram of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 further showed that C and N elements were densely distributed, while O and Fe elements were relatively sparsely distributed, indicating that the composites were mainly composed of P-CN/NCDs and a small amount of Fe3O4.

Increasing the intensity and expanding the range of light absorption was the effective way to accelerate the utilization rate of visible light. The optical properties of P-CN, P-CN/NCDs, and P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were characterized by UV-Vis DRS ( Fig. 4a). Pure Fe3O4 showed good optical response to incident light from 250 to 800 nm. This was due to its black color and inability to show the absorption fringes, indicating its metallic nature. When Fe3O4 was introduced, the optical absorption of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was red-shifted in the visible region (400–800 nm). The broadening of the absorption edge of these materials from 444 nm and 514 nm might be attributed to the upconversion properties of NCDs and the effective combination of Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs [41]. P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 displayed stronger light absorption intensity, which contributed to its better degradation performance. The bandgap energies of all photophotocatalysts, including Fe3O4, were derived by the following equation.
( α h v ) = A
( h v − E g )
n / 2
where n denotes the transition characteristics, such as direct (bare Fe3O4, n = 4) and indirect (bare P-CN, P-CN/NCDs and P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4, n = 1),while ɑ, Eg, h, and ν denote the absorption coefficient, band gap energy, Planck constant, and light frequency respectively [14,15].

The corresponding band gaps (Eg) for P-CN, P-CN/NCDs, and P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were 2.71 eV, 2.67 eV and 2.55 eV, respectively. To further understand the relationship between the carrier density (ND) and the flat band potential (EFB), Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots were obtained by fitting the x-axis intercept of the linear part of the 1/C2 vs. potential, as shown in Fig. S8. Based on the slope type of the M-S plots, PCN, P-CN/NCDs, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 were judged to be typical n-type semiconductors, and the flat-band potentials of PCN, P-CN/NCDs, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 were −1.51 V, −1.14 V, −0.9 V and −0.7 V, respectively (relative to Ag/AgCl, pH = 7), which corresponds to −1.31 V, −0.94 V, −0.7 V and −0.5 V relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE). The UV–vis DRS results showed that P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 exhibited the strongest visible light absorption and the smallest band gap. Therefore, it may have more photogenerated carriers and exhibit higher catalytic activity under visible light irradiation [38,39]. The composite P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 displayed significantly enhanced absorption in the visible region, which might be attributed to the mutual synergistic effect of the narrow bandgap and light absorption properties of Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs. These results indicated that P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 significantly improved the light absorption properties through heterojunction and increased the conversion efficiency of sunlight, which contributed to the improvement of photocatalytic activity. This was because they were active in a wide range of visible light regions and could generate charge carriers under visible light irradiation. XPS valence band spectra were used to investigate the energy band structure of the photocatalysts. The valence bands (VB) of PCN, P-CN/NCDs, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 were determined to be 1.64 eV, 1.97 eV, 2.09 eV and 2.82 eV, as shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. S9d. Therefore, the value of the valence band relative to the NHE (EVB-NHE, relative to the NHE, pH = 7) can be calculated according to the following equation: EVB-NHE = φ+EVB-XPS-4.44 EVB-NHE is the VB potential relative to NHE (pH = 7), φ is the electron power function of the XPS analyzer (4.2 eV), and EVB-XPS is the VB value determined from the VB-XPS curve. Therefore, the EVB-NHE for PCN, P-CN/NCDs, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 were calculated to be 1.40 eV, 1.73 eV, 1.85 eV and 2.58 eV, respectively. Combined with the band gap values of the materials, the CB values of PCN, P-CN/NCDs, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 were −1.31, −0.94, −0.70 and −0.50 V with respect to NHE, respectively. This was consistent with the Mott-Schottky plot (Fig. S8).

Further validation of lower photogenerated carrier complexation rates in heterojunctions was provided by PL spectroscopy, transient fluorescence, transient photocurrent and EIS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4c, the P-CN/NCDs exhibit lower fluorescence intensity and significant blueshift compared with P-CN. This might be attributed to the fact that the doped NCDs effectively prolong the lifetime of the photogenerated carriers, the charge transfer rate, and the quantum confinement effect due to the change in the density of electronic states. The fluorescence emission intensity of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was lower than that of P-CN and P-CN/NCDs, which could be attributed to the inhibition of photogenerated electron-hole pair complexation by Fe3O4 doping. In addition, the PL emission intensity of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was lower than that of P-CN/Fe3O4, which indicated that the introduction of NCDs improved the charge separation, and the NCDs could act as an electronic mediator to direct electron transfer at the P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 interface [50]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4d, the quantum lifetime of the photogenerated carriers was estimated by time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra. The ternary composite P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 photocatalyst showed faster decay kinetics than pure P-CN. This suggested that the charge separation ability of P-CN was enhanced when NCDs quantum dots and Fe3O4 were double doped together. The charge lifetime of P-CN was 1.84 ns, where as that of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was as high as 5.69 ns, which indicated that the separation of the electron-hole pairs was more efficient at the S-type interface. It was further confirmed that the heterojunction structure effectively accelerated the charge separation and transfer process [49].

The prolongation of lifetimes originated from the radiative complexation of carriers, which should be the main reason for the prolongation of carrier lifetimes in heterojunctions. We confirmed the formation of heterojunctions between Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs by measuring photocurrents to determine the rate of separation of light-generated electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 4e, the order of current densities was P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4> P-CN/NCDs> P-CN. This suggested that the interfacial charge transfer rate of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was faster, which resulted in more electrons. The transient photocurrent response curves of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 were very sensitive to the switching on and off of visible light and had good stability. The significantly enhanced photocurrent density also indicated a low complexation rate of photogenerated carriers. It was seen that the photocurrent intensity of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was the highest, which was the result of the co-modification of the heterojunction and NCDs. The photogenerated carrier lifetime of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was effectively prolonged and the separation of electron-hole pairs was significantly enhanced, which was consistent with the results of PL analysis. The reduction of the diameter of the EIS semicircular arc proved the superior electron transfer capability of the P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 heterojunction. Meanwhile, the irradiation of visible light further accelerated the charge transfer process of the samples, which proved that light irradiation could generate more photogenerated electrons. The results showed that a heterojunction was formed between Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs, and the complexation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes was suppressed. The composites had higher electrical conductivity and electron affinity, which could effectively promote charge transfer and separation and enhance the photo-Fenton reaction activity [42]. These results were also consistent with the experimental validation of the photocatalytic reaction.

The optimal ratios of the binary composites were firstly screened and P-CN/NCDs-2 was obtained as the optimal ratio (Fig. S11). Then the optimal dosing ratio for the addition of Fe3O4 was determined (Fig. S12). The influencing factors of catalyst P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 on the photo-Fenton degradation of LEV were investigated through a series of experiments, including the initial solution concentration (Fig. S13a), the catalyst dosage (Fig. S13b), and the pH (Fig. S13c). Through the screening of the above reaction conditions, we obtained the optimal ratios and suitable reaction conditions. The subsequent experiments were carried out under these optimal conditions. In order to investigate the temperature sensitivity of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4, degradation was carried out at different temperatures (from 0 °C 25 °C to 50 °C). The results in Fig. 5a indicated that the photocatalytic degradation performance of the resulting photocatalyst P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was slightly affected by temperature. In particular, the photocatalytic activity was slightly enhanced at higher temperatures, which could be attributed to the enhanced molecular motion at higher temperatures, exposing more active sites. The presence of coexisting anions and natural organic compounds may affect the photocatalytic degradation of levofloxacin (LEV). Therefore, several representative anions (H2PO4 -, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, and Cl-) and humic acids (HA) in the presence of natural water were selected to explore the effect of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 on the photocatalytic degradation of LEV (the concentrations of all anions and HA were set to 20 mM). Fig. 5b showed that the degradation rate increased significantly because SO4 2- consumed •OH to produce •SO4 -, captured electrons, and inhibited the recombination of e--h+ pairs. Cl- had a scavenging effect on h+ and slightly increased the removal rate of LEV. NO3 - trapped •OH and h+, resulting in lower photodegradation efficiency. CO3 2- could increase the pH value and alkalize the LEV solution with its pH buffering effect, which would reduce the photodegradation efficiency of LEV. HA could also act as a photosensitizer to produce active substances under sunlight or simulated sunlight irradiation, so the degradation efficiency was higher. Fig. 5c showed that the degradation of LEV was due to the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and Fenton oxidation, and P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 could generate H2O2 autonomously. Comparative tests were carried out under these conditions (Fig. 5d), the order of photocatalytic degradation of LEVs was P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 > P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4-s > P-CN/NCDs > P-CN/Fe3O4 > P-CN > Fe3O4, which was consistent with the results of photocurrent analysis. The highest photocatalytic LEV degradation activity of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was observed from the comparative test (Fig. 5d), reaching 81.4 % in the range of 75 minutes. This indicated that P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 had good catalytic performance. The photosensitization of the blank control group was negligible. The adsorption capacity of Fe3O4 showed less difference and almost no catalytic performance in catalytic degradation due to the lack of photoactive components. After composite with P-CN, the photocatalytic activity of P-CN/Fe3O4 increased by 69.3 %. This was attributed to the rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in Fe3O4 and P-CN. The Fenton reaction could also occur with P-CN/Fe3O4, but its charge transfer capacity and catalytic ability (69.3 %) were much weaker than that of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 (81.4 %). The ternary composite P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 showed excellent photo-Fenton degradation performance. This was attributed to the synergistic effect of Fe3O4 and NCDs, which acted as mediators of electrons and could effectively accelerate the charge separation to produce more •OH by photocatalytic H2O2. The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of LEV was studied using a pseudo-second-order model (Fig. S16), and the results showed that the degradation rate constant of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was 0.04234 min−1, which was 5.6 times that of CN. This suggested a synergistic effect of the heterojunction formed between Fe3O4 and P-CN/NCDs. The superior photocatalytic ability of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 might be due to the enhanced electron transfer and light absorption between P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4, both of which could promote the photo-Fenton reaction to produce more •OH. To illustrate the photocatalytic ability of this material, Table 2 compared some previous LEV photocatalytic systems with current research. Fig. 5e indicated that the material had good photocatalytic degradation performance for antibiotics. The ease of separation was investigated by the hysteresis loop of the catalyst P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 recovered after photo-Fenton catalyzed degradation (Fig. S17a). The symmetric hysteresis loop and high magnetization saturation value (15.90 emu g−1) facilitated the separation and reuse of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4. Under the conditions of an external magnetic field, the photo-Fenton-catalyzed degradation of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was easily separated, indicating that the magnetic samples had good magnetic recovery and separation performance. The photo-Fenton degradation cycle was carried out on LEV to investigate the structure and degradation stability of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4. After each cyclic experiment, the ternary catalyst was separated by an external magnetic field and moved to the next cyclic test cycle. The degradation efficiency of LEV remained at 79.4 % after 4 degradation cycles, confirming the superior stability of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 in the photo-Fenton reaction (Fig. 5f).

As shown in Fig. 5g-h, no changes were detected in the XRD and FTIR results after the degradation cycle, indicating that the structure and composition of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 remained unchanged with good structural stability. The stability of the catalyst at high temperatures was one of the desired properties of the catalyst. Fig. S17b showed the results of the TG analysis. It was found that there was very little weight loss at temperatures up to 520 °C. Therefore, the prepared P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was considered to be a stable catalyst in the high-temperature range. Combined with the Total Organic Carbon (TOC) content test (Fig. S17c), it could be seen that the TOC value of the antibiotic decreased from the initial 82.7–47.2 %, which indicated that most organic molecules were mineralized during the degradation process.

In this paper, the degradation intermediates and residues of LEV were further analyzed by LC-MS detection studies. The structural information of the intermediates was listed in Table S2 and the mass spectra of some of the substances are shown in Fig. S18-S23. The three main routes of LEV degradation through carboxylation/decarboxylation, demethylation, and depiperazination were analyzed [56–58]. The possible degradation pathways were speculated in the literature, and all intermediates were finally degraded to small molecules of H2O, CO2, F-, NO3 -, NH4 +, and so on ( Fig. 6).

The mechanism of LEV degradation by P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was hypothesized by free radical capture experiments and EPR tests. Fig. 7a showed the effect of different capture agents on the degradation rate of LEV, BQ>IPA>EDTA-2Na. The electron spin resonance (EPR) technique was further used to demonstrate the active substances in the degradation. Both results indicated that •O2 - and •OH played a major role in the photocatalytic degradation.

Based on the analyses of PL, photocurrent curves, and electrochemical impedance diagrams, DMPO-OH and DMPO-O2 - were generated using the EPR technique with DMPO as the trapping agent, respectively. The charge transfer mechanism in the photocatalytic process was further explored by evaluating the generation of •OH and •O2 - ( Fig. 8a-b). P-CN/NCDs present a weak characteristic signal of DMPO-OH, and the DMPO-•OH signal was stronger in P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4, suggesting that there was no transfer of photogenerated h+ on the VB of Fe3O4. This phenomenon was inconsistent with the conventional charge transfer pattern of type II heterojunctions. In addition, the DMPO-•O2 - signal of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was stronger, suggesting that the photogenerated e- stayed on the CB of P-CN/NCDs and did not migrate to the CB of Fe3O4. The DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 - had significantly enhanced EPR signals, which could be attributed to the formation of heterogeneous junctions [59–62]. Through VB-XPS and UV–vis DRS tests, the conduction and valence band potentials of P-CN were found to be −1.07 eV and 1.64 eV, respectively, and those of Fe3O4 were found to be −0.26 eV and 2.82 eV [48]. As observed in Fig. S9a, a positive photocurrent existed at 425 nm under photoexcitation, exhibiting typical n-type semiconductor effects. Weak excitation existed at 500 nm and 550 nm. It could be demonstrated that the iron tetraoxide material at a wavelength of 420 nm was able to produce the separation of electron and hole pairs. When P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was exposed to visible light, photogenerated e- could be excited and transferred from the VB of P-CN and Fe3O4 to the CB, leaving h+ in the VB. As the conduction band potential of P-CN (ECB= −1.07 eV vs. NHE) was higher than that of O2 (O2/•O2 - = −0.33 eV vs. NHE), it was suggested that the resulting photoexcited electrons could trap O2 and produce •O2 -. The OH-/ •OH potential (2.40 eV vs. NHE) was larger than the valence band potential of P-CN (1.64 eV vs. NHE) but smaller than that of Fe3O4 (2.82 eV vs. NHE). However, •OH and •O2 - were proved to be the dominant ROS. Thus the assumptions of the type II heterojunction model were invalid in the P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 heterojunction (Fig. 8c). This was consistent with the conclusions extrapolated above. In contrast, S-scheme heterojunctions might be a correct charge transfer configuration to explain enhanced catalytic activity on P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 heterojunctions (Fig. 8d).

Based on the above analysis, the Eg of Fe3O4 and P-CN are 3.08 eV and 2.71 eV, respectively. The ECB and EVB of the as-prepared materials were determined using VB-XPS and UV photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). The work functions of Fe3O4, NCDs and P-CN are 6.52, 3.18 and 5.22 eV, respectively ( Fig. 9a). The energy gaps from Fermi energy level to the VB maximum for Fe3O4 and P-CN energy gaps from the Fermi energy level to the VB maximum are 2.82 eV and 1.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 9b-c). Therefore, the energy levels (Δ) of the VBM in Fe3O4 and P-CN were calculated to be 9.34 eV and 6.86 eV, respectively. In addition, the CB was calculated as ECB = EVB - Eg, and the CB of Fe3O4 and P-CN were 6.26 eV and 4.15 eV, respectively. Finally, the energy gaps between the VBM and the VB maxima in Fe3O4 and P-CN were calculated to be 0.26 eV and 1.07 eV, respectively, using the reversible hydrogen electrode potentials (RHE, 0 V vs. −4.44 eV), the energy gaps between Fe3O4 (EVB: 2.82 eV, ECB: −0.5 eV), and P-CN (EVB: 1.40 eV, ECB: −1.31 eV) for normalization. The Fermi energy level difference between P-CN, NCDs and Fe3O4 leads to the built-in electric field. Under the effect of the built-in electric field, the energy band of P-CN was bent upward and that of Fe3O4 was bent downward, forming an electron transfer path from the conduction band (CB) of Fe3O4 through NCDs to the valence band (VB) of P-CN. In addition, the tight interfaces connected by van der Waals forces further accelerated the electron flow from Fe3O4 to P-CN. Ultimately, spatial separation of photogenerated carriers was achieved. The electrons and holes with strong redox capabilities were retained and applied in the photocatalytic reaction, respectively. The direction of charge transfer in the P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 heterojunction was different from that in the conventional type II heterojunction. The built-in electric field (IEF) at the interface greatly affects the separation and complexation process of photogenerated electrons and holes. Eventually, electrons in the valence band of P-CN and Fe3O4 were excited to jump to their conduction band (CB) under visible light irradiation. Tight interfacial interactions, stronger IEF, and energy band bending effects together promote the complexation of electron pathway NCDs produced by Fe3O4 with holes produced by P-CN, consuming carriers with weaker redox capabilities. The retained electrons and holes were efficiently spatially separated in the conduction band of P-CN with strong redox capacity and the valence band of Fe3O4.

Therefore, a possible energy band structure of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was proposed. Fe3O4 and P-CN had a staggered energy band structure (Fig. 9d-g). Upon contact, the electrons at the interface move from P-CN and NCDs (higher Fermi energy level) to Fe3O4 (lower Fermi energy level) until the Fermi energy level reached equilibrium. During this process, the NCDs act as charge transfer channels located between Fe3O4 and P-CN, facilitating the rapid transfer of electrons. The depletion of electrons on the P-CN side of the interface and the accumulation of electrons on the Fe3O4 side created a built-in electric field (IEF) along Fe3O4 to P-CN, which produced energy band bending. Therefore, under the influence of IEF, energy band bending, and Coulomb interactions, e- in Fe3O4CB was more readily complexed with h+ in P-CN VB through charge transport channels (NCDs) to form S-type heterojunctions (Fig. 9g). The photogenerated e--h+ pairs in Fe3O4 and P-CN were efficiently separated, resulting in the retention of more reduced e- in the CB of Fe3O4 and more oxidized h+ in the VB of P-CN, which enhanced the photocatalytic ability of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4. More importantly, the remaining h+ of Fe3O4 in VB and the e- of P-CN in CB could react with H2O and O2 to form •OH and •O2 -, respectively, since the constructed reaction system belongs to a non-homogeneous phase-like Fenton-like process. Thus, the photogenerated electrons effectively accelerated the conversion of Fe(III) to Fe(II) and the redox cycle of Fe(III)/Fe(II) favored the formation of more •OH by the addition of hydrogen peroxide. The decomposition of H2O2 also consumed the light-generated e- to produce •OH, which also contributed to the inhibition of carrier recombination, and thus more h+ might be directly involved in the oxidation of LEV. Thus, the formation of S-type heterojunctions was confirmed by PL, photocurrent density, electrochemical impedance, EPR results and analyzing the figure of merit.

Therefore, we could reasonably propose a possible reaction mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of LEV by P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4. EVB (+2.09 eV) was lower than •OH/H2O (+2.8 eV), while ECB (-0.46 eV) was lower than O2/•O2 - (-0.33 eV). Thus, P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 directly produced •O2 - during catalysis. Moreover, •O2 - could be converted to H2O2 (0.895 eV). The photocatalytic mechanism of LEV degradation by P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was shown in Fig. 10. For LEV photodegradation, large amounts of separated e- and h+ were produced by P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 under visible light irradiation. h+ could directly participate in the oxidative decomposition process of LEV, while e- could reduce dissolved O2 to form •O2 –, which then oxidatively decomposes LEV. In summary, the generation of •O2 -, •OH, and h+ with strong oxidative capacity could effectively degrade LEV, and the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and heterophase Fenton reaction could be achieved on S-scheme heterojunctions.
Visible light + P-CN→ h+ + e-
H2O + h+ → •OH + H+
O2 + e-→ •O2 -
2•OH → H2O2
Fe3+ + e-→ Fe2+
Fe2+ + H2O2 → OH- + •OH +Fe3+
e- + H2O2 → OH- + •OH
•OH + •O2 -+ h+ + LEV → small molecules

In summary, the ternary nanocomposite photocatalytic material P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was successfully prepared by combining the hydrothermal method and co-precipitation method. The experimental results showed that the recombination of P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4 led to enhanced absorption in the visible light region and also presented an excellent photocatalytic antibiotic degradation efficiency, which could reach 81.4 % within 75 min. The improved performance of the photo-Fenton-like properties could be attributed to the wide visible light adsorption region and the efficient formation of heterojunctions originating from the energy level matching between P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4. The internal electric field established at the interface of P-CN/NCDs and Fe3O4 improved the spatial separation and transfer characteristics of photo-generated carriers. The accelerated transfer of electrons promoted the regeneration of Fe2+, which further enhanced the photocatalytic-Fenton reaction. Experiments confirmed that the above synergy was the main reason for improving the photocatalytic activity. In addition, P-CN/BC/NCDs had sound catalytic activity for the degradation of chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), doxycycline (DOX) and other antibiotics. Repeated experimental studies and structural analyses showed that P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 was stable and could be reused many times. Free radical trapping experiments and EPR tests confirmed that the main active substances in the catalytic degradation of LEV under light conditions were •O2 - and •OH. In conclusion, the synergistic effect of P-CN/NCDs/Fe3O4 could be exerted to improve the photocatalytic performance of materials and provide ideas for the design and practical application of high-performance photocatalysts.

Heping Li: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xiaoyan Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis. Wanyi Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaoling Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Qi Guo: Investigation, Formal analysis. Meng Wen: Software, Investigation. Haijuan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
